My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

input.initialPrompt: My ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app running on a Windows Server has slow requests under load. I have admin access. Which trace should I collect to capture waits/blocks, and what PerfView arguments should I use?

data.content:
## Collecting a Thread-Time ETW Trace with PerfView

For diagnosing waits/blocks (thread stalls, lock contention, async delays), collect a **Thread Time** trace. This captures both on-CPU and **off-CPU** (blocked/waiting) time via context-switch events.

### PerfView command

```powershell
PerfView.exe collect /ThreadTime /NoGui /AcceptEULA /MaxCollectSec:30 /CircularMB:1024 /Zip:True
```

### Key flags explained

| Flag | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `/ThreadTime` | Enables context-switch + readying-thread events so you see **why** a thread was blocked and **what** woke it up |
| `/MaxCollectSec:30` | Limits collection to 30 s — reproduce the slow request during this window |
| `/CircularMB:1024` | 1 GB circular buffer; prevents trace truncation under load |
| `/Zip:True` | Produces a single `.etl.zip` for easy transfer |
| `/NoGui` | Headless — safe for production servers |

### Analysis

Open the `.etl.zip` in PerfView and use **Thread Time Stacks** → pick your `dotnet` (or `w3wp`) process. Group by `[Thread Frame]` to see where threads spent time **waiting** (e.g., `BLOCKED_TIME`, `System.Threading.Monitor.Wait`, async `Task` awaits, socket reads, etc.).

> **Tip:** If you also want GC and allocation details, add `/GCCollectOnly` or `/ClrEvents:GC` to the same command.
data.reasoningText: The user is asking about performance tracing for an ASP.NET Core (.NET 8) app on Windows Server. They want to know which trace to collect for waits/blocks and the specific PerfView arguments. This is a knowledge question — no tools needed.
